I am inviting the community to review the last 7 pages of the 'NumPy Contributor Comics', which is part of the GSOD-NumPy 2024 project. If this looks familiar, that’s because this work is a continuation of the GSOD 2023 work. The comic can be viewed here, as a PDF (to upload link). You can [comment directly on the PDF viewer](https://drive.google.com/file/d/18-CWOIoBjMrffwMuKsx2YRkY9hkJm-dM/view?usp=drive_link) or on this Github issue. These comics aim to show newcomers that NumPy is driven by people who use it: in this case, scientists! These comics will show grad students finding a usability problem in NumPy, working together to submit an issue, and talking to community members. This thread will be open for the next two weeks. **For Review:** - For review, you can comment on any part, such as re-wording a specific line or panel. - I recommend jumping to review pages 11-16 and the back cover, since that was completed this year. **Some major changes since last review:** - Remove from references of PyData NYC 2023 edition - This part was added when I gave a talk about the grant at PyData NYC - Add other characters, such as the contributor and maintainer - Show the process of reviewing PRs, merging and release cycle - Add a credit section to name original contributors that made the .npz filetype change","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27375/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27375/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2397654180","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27375#issuecomment-2397654180","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27375","id":2397654180,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86O6Uyk","user":{"login":"MarsBarLee","id":46167686,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTY3Njg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46167686?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee","html_url":"https://github.com/MarsBarLee","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-07T18:53:50Z","updated_at":"2024-10-07T18:53:50Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"@eagunn Hi Anne, thanks for pointing that out, that was something I missed out on set-up. I welcome translations of the comic! I really appreciate that people want to share the comic through translation! I haven't fully figured out the translatoin process but I can create a tracking issue for translation and ping you on there. **My understanding of the translation process:** - Translate the [English script](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1bXwHd8F-0zFMeCu9ohASynmyOY03cmZHNTOD2Sp2oC0/edit?usp=sharing) into Portuguese (note: this is not the final script but close to final) - Review the translation - Update the text in a graphics editor (such as GIMP, Inkscape or Krita) - Review the typesetting (does the translated text fit in the speech bubbles?) - Release translated comic **Items** - Comic script in English - Comic pages with blank speech bubbles - Comic script in Portuguese - Comic pages with Portugeuse **Some considerations:** - Will there be enough space in the speech bubbles to fit the translated text? - The speech bubbles were sized in English but might be too small or big for Portuguese text - This is a common problem in Japanese to English manga translation where English text may take up 20% more space than Japanese and thus not fit the speech bubbles. But perhaps not a problem here since both English and Portuguese use the Roman alphabet - The current font, Anime Ace, has letters for English but I'm not sure if it has all the letters for Portuguese. We may need to select a new font that has all the letter for Portuguese","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2397754809/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

comic","user":{"login":"MarsBarLee","id":46167686,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTY3Njg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46167686?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee","html_url":"https://github.com/MarsBarLee","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1624869342,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjI0ODY5MzQy","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/labels/translations","name":"translations","color":"207de5","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-14T00:54:58Z","updated_at":"2024-10-14T00:54:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi all, this is a tracking isuse on translating the "How to Contribute to Numpy" comic. These comics aim to show newcomers that NumPy is driven by people who use it: in this case, scientists! These comics will show grad students finding a usability problem in NumPy, working together to submit an issue, and talking to community members. Here's [a link to the comic](https://heyzine.com/flip-book/3e66a13901.html). This is following similar efforts to translate the NumPy documentation at #55 ## Timeline I will be able to help with translation after December, since I am finishing up the comic before then. ## Languages - We'll probably start with a Portuguese translation. - @joao-antonio-gg expressed interest in translating the comics to Portuguese in a [previous issue about the comics](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27375) - I welcome other languages as well! Just comment here with your interest and language. ## Process - Translate the [English script](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1bXwHd8F-0zFMeCu9ohASynmyOY03cmZHNTOD2Sp2oC0/edit?usp=sharing) into the target language (note: this is not the final script but close to final) - Review the translation - Update the text in a graphics editor (such as GIMP, Inkscape or Krita) - Review the typesetting (does the translated text fit in the speech bubbles?) - Release translated comic **Items:** - Comic script in English - Comic pages with blank speech bubbles - Comic script in target language - Comic pages with target language","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/789/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/789/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

I am inviting the community to review the last 7 pages of the 'NumPy Contributor Comics', which is part of the GSOD-NumPy 2024 project. If this looks familiar, that’s because this work is a continuation of the GSOD 2023 work. The comic can be viewed here, as a PDF (to upload link). You can [comment directly on the PDF viewer](https://drive.google.com/file/d/18-CWOIoBjMrffwMuKsx2YRkY9hkJm-dM/view?usp=drive_link) or on this Github issue. These comics aim to show newcomers that NumPy is driven by people who use it: in this case, scientists! all, this is a tracking isuse on translating the "How to Contribute to Numpy" comic. These comics aim to show newcomers that NumPy is driven by people who use it: in this case, scientists! These comics will show grad students finding a usability problem in NumPy, working together to submit an issue, and talking to community members. Here's [a link to the comic](https://heyzine.com/flip-book/3e66a13901.html). This is following similar efforts to translate the NumPy documentation at #55 ## Timeline I will be able to help with translation after December, since I am finishing up the comic before then. ## Languages - We'll probably start with a Portuguese translation. - @joao-antonio-gg expressed interest in translating the comics to Portuguese in a [previous issue about the comics](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27375) - I welcome other languages as well! Just comment here with your interest and language. ## Process - Translate the [English script](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1bXwHd8F-0zFMeCu9ohASynmyOY03cmZHNTOD2Sp2oC0/edit?usp=sharing) into the target language (note: this is not the final script but close to final) - Review the translation - Update the text in a graphics editor (such as GIMP, Inkscape or Krita) - Review the typesetting (does the translated text fit in the speech bubbles?) - Release translated comic **Items:** - I am inviting the community to review the first 10 pages of the '**NumPy Contributor Comics**', which is part of the GSOD-NumPy 2023 project.' **The comic can be [viewed here, as a PDF](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1F1pLhpVNRuMnVdmZ50QmeDMCDT0rdTFo/view?usp=sharing)**. You can comment direct on the PDF viewer or on this Github issue. Update since [Community Review #2](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/24382): Illustrated of first 10 pages, based on the script. This version of the comics was released at the PyData NYC 2023 conference. I gave a talk there- [here’s the video of the talk](https://youtu.be/Gv_Ea94wquM?si=drLkjdN_0A75Jshn&t=40). These comics aim to show newcomers that NumPy is driven by people who use it: in this case, scientists! These comics will show grad students finding a usability problem in NumPy, working together to submit an issue, and talking to community members. For more details, check out the [project roadmap](https://medium.com/@marsbarlee/gsod-numpy-contributor-comics-project-roadmap-521280503fbd). I will also host a **review session at the next NumPy Community Meeting on December 6**, if you would like to chat! This thread will be open for the next two weeks and **close on December 20.** ### For Review: For review, you can comment on any part, such as re-wording a specific line or panel. Note: The comic has 10/16 pages complete. The remaining 6 pages will illustrate the script. Some specific parts I intend to add or change in the future: **Adapting from PyData NYC 2023 edition to numpy.org version** - Currently there are references to PyData NYC (on front cover and back cover) - I plan on removing these references - Replace the QR code on a page to lead to the numpy.org comic version **Next 6 pages** - Last panel of the comic on Page 10 explains the relationship between maintainers and contributors - Expand on this relationship in following pages - A student will ask “Why doesn’t the maintainer do everything?” - Answered by a visual metaphor of multiple people in a campsite working together. The maintainer as the lead organizer, and each doing a different job **Credits** - Add a 'Based on a true story': links to real [Github issue](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/23319) and [PR](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23357) the comic was based on","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25318/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25318/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

The maintainer as the lead organizer, and each doing a different job **Credits** - Add a 'Based on a true story': links to real [Github issue](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/23319) and [PR](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23357) the comic was based on","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25318/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/25318/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/46167686/537d3a9d-e8f5-4844-b3e6-3a528ee0116a)

Hi everyone! I am inviting the community to review the script of the '**NumPy Contributor Comics**', which is the of the GSOD-NumPy 2023 project.'

**The script can be viewed here, as a [Google Doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fjLTDqSkcKMxo8oSTRThllBFpjo5rXS9/edit?usp=sharing&ouid=110556856523158639329&rtpof=true&sd=true)**. You can comment either here on Github or on the Google Doc, whichever you prefer.

These comics aim to show newcomers that NumPy is driven by people who use it: in this case, scientists! These comics will show grad students finding a usability problem in NumPy,  working together to submit an issue, and talking to community members.

For more details, check out the [project roadmap](https://medium.com/@marsbarlee/gsod-numpy-contributor-comics-project-roadmap-521280503fbd).

I will also host a **review session at the next NumPy Community Meeting on August 16**, if you would like to chat!
This thread will be open for the next two weeks and **close on August 23**.

### Idea or request for content:

For review, you can comment on any part, whether you like the idea of the comics, don't like a specific line or not sure what something means.

Some specific parts I would like feedback on:
- **Themes**
  - [On Page 5, ](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fjLTDqSkcKMxo8oSTRThllBFpjo5rXS9/edit?disco=AAAA3sVZK9Y)does this panel accurately show the tie between open source and open science?
- **Story and Pacing**
  - Does this seem like a reasonable amount of content to cover in 16 pages?
  - Do the three students interacting together feel realistic?
    - eg Since they're beginners, they don't at answer immediately
    - eg not too mean-spirited? They argue, to show different approaches
- **Accuracy**
  - [On Page 3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fjLTDqSkcKMxo8oSTRThllBFpjo5rXS9/edit?disco=AAAA3sVZK9c), how accurate is the .npz code?
  - [On Page 7](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fjLTDqSkcKMxo8oSTRThllBFpjo5rXS9/edit?disco=AAAA3sVZK9k), does the visual metaphor of the NumPy issue tracker as a bulletin board make sense?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24382/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":2,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24382/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1714732233","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/24382#issuecomment-1714732233","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/24382","id":1714732233,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP85mNLzJ","user":{"login":"MarsBarLee","id":46167686,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTY3Njg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46167686?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee","html_url":"https://github.com/MarsBarLee","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-11T23:22:53Z","updated_at":"2023-09-11T23:22:53Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Hi @w1ck-3d, thank you for reviewing and your comment! I’m glad that the visual metaphors on page 5 and 7 make sense. I also like the suggestion about using logos to transition between scenes. Perhaps to update the old-school and avoid copyrights, we could make a parody of the Switch logo, with two snakes...? Not too far a stretch since the Python logo has two snakes, just like how the Switch logo has two controllers.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1714732233/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":600,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/pulls/600","id":1056373221,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAA6rk84-9vnl","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy.org/pull/600","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy.org/pull/600.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy.org/pull/600.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/600","number":600,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"WIP: Contributor Journey Comics","user":{"login":"MarsBarLee","id":46167686,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTY3Njg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46167686?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee","html_url":"https://github.com/MarsBarLee","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"![Numpy_Adventure](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46167686/190224255-c6a4416d-a900-4688-b953-a09d91ace203.png)
# To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy
- Designers, especially from [Open Source # To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy
- Designers, especially from [Open Source # To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy
- Designers, especially from [Open Source # To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy

# To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy
- Designers, especially from [Open Source # To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy

# To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy
- Designers, especially from [Open Source # To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy
- Designers, especially from [Open Source # To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy
- Designers, especially from [Open Source # To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy

# To comment on this, use Github Suggestions or comments (some examples below).

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto a commonly used platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy
- Designers, especially from [Open Source # To give feedback, use Github Suggestions or Github comments (some examples below).
- Check out https://deploy-preview-600--numpy-org.netlify.app/contribute/ to see the live page!
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46167686/190227370-1c93075d-a413-47be-8b3f-7476162ea50f.png)
- Then, go to '[Files changed](https://github.com/numpy/numpy.org/pull/600/files)' to comment on what could be different

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto Github, a popular platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy
- awkward formatting! The image, image description and script are one long line, as they are one Markdown table row. This long line makes it harder to parse and give feedback to.

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))
- existing design tools for collaboration, such as [Figma](https://www.figma.com/file/7cVUnr5f4EG6yCRn2GJVQX/Untitled?node-id=0%3A1)
![ZzGtp9i](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46167686/190227565-0766ec30-71f1-42e6-a62f-a79ab0ddee83.png)

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy
- Designers, especially from [Open Source Design](https://discourse.opensourcedesign.net/t/open-source-comics-resources-collaboration-and-funding-for-making-comics-for-open-source-projects/3130/4)

# How I am inviting people
- Mars will go to various community calls (NumPy, NumPy Docs, Open Source Design) for live, verbal feedback
- Call attendees are invited to write their comment here
- Mars will also post consolidated meeting notes and discussion points from these meetings","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/600/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/600/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/comments/1258088876","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy.org/pull/600#issuecomment-1258088876","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/600","id":1258088876,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA6rk85K_Oms","user":{"login":"MarsBarLee","id":46167686,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTY3Njg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46167686?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee","html_url":"https://github.com/MarsBarLee","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-26T14:01:36Z","updated_at":"2022-09-26T14:01:36Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Feedback and Suggestion from NumPy Community Call ([link to archived notes](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-09-14.md))
- Zoom out to bigger map with non-code contribution journey
  - Could be a separate map, to not over-complicate the current map
-  Concerns of depicting hierarchy
  - Instead of going up like a mountain, maybe a flat plane like a forest? With different paths in the forest
  - Inspiration blog post: [Imposter Syndrome in Data Science](https://www.caitlinhudon.com/posts/2018/01/19/imposter-syndrome-in-data-science)
    - Expanding spheres of knowledge and influence
    - Continue fire metaphor but rather than put torch to central fire, every person carries own torch and bringing together with other people makes a brighter fire, sphere
    - Horizontal collaboration
- What if people have different mental models and want to be represented differently?
  - The model we have in reality vs the model we want to move to
  - Maybe show both models? To compare
- Making invisible process visible
- Get more feedback across projects
  - maybe through SPEC?

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46167686/190227370-1c93075d-a413-47be-8b3f-7476162ea50f.png)
- Then, go to '[Files changed](https://github.com/numpy/numpy.org/pull/600/files)' to comment on what could be different

Continuing the discussion from the [NumPy Documentation meeting](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://hackmd.io/oB_boakvRqKR-_2jRV-Qjg&sa=D&source=calendar&ust=1663605461230540&usg=AOvVaw0A2aV9bsdJkikJNZhOd-dK), this PR exists to
- invite non-designers into the design process
- by bringing images onto Github, a popular platform for open source work and discussion

---

# The advantages of this method is:
- lessen barriers of entry to non-designers
  - e.g instead of linking out to a Figma file and requiring developers to make a Figma account
- work on asynchronously
- have version control
- to centralize discussion of this topic across NumPy community calls

# What could be bettered or being discussed is:
- the workload imbalance, where the designer must do some additional work to translate their work and processes 
- where this comic could live in numpy.org/the internet. This is currently placed in contributing.md
- how this comic could be expanded and shared to other projects, once we figure out this process specifically for NumPy
- awkward formatting! The image, image description and script are one long line, as they are one Markdown table row. This long line makes it harder to parse and give feedback to.

# The inspiration for this 
- discussion of contributor → maintainer, from the NumPy community call on August 31, 2022. ([meeting notes with details](https://github.com/numpy/archive/blob/main/community_meetings/community-2022-08-31.md#new-topics))
- existing design tools for collaboration, such as [Figma](https://www.figma.com/file/7cVUnr5f4EG6yCRn2GJVQX/Untitled?node-id=0%3A1)
![ZzGtp9i](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46167686/190227565-0766ec30-71f1-42e6-a62f-a79ab0ddee83.png)

# Who I am inviting to this review
- NumPy contributors and anyone who has opinions on sustaining NumPy
- Designers, especially from [Open Source Design](https://discourse.opensourcedesign.net/t/open-source-comics-resources-collaboration-and-funding-for-making-comics-for-open-source-projects/3130/4)

# How I am inviting people
- Mars will go to various community calls (NumPy, NumPy Docs, Open Source Design) for live, verbal feedback
- Call attendees are invited to write their comment here
- Mars will also post consolidated meeting notes and discussion points from these meetings","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/600/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/600/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/comments/1258157615","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy.org/pull/600#issuecomment-1258157615","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/600","id":1258157615,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA6rk85K_fYv","user":{"login":"MarsBarLee","id":46167686,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTY3Njg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46167686?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee","html_url":"https://github.com/MarsBarLee","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-26T14:50:16Z","updated_at":"2022-09-26T14:50:16Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Feedback and Suggestion from NumPy Newcomers Design Jam on Sept 22
- Link to [Jamboard](https://jamboard.google.com/d/10dIw7ptXrFwPpHin1DgMQsi5i-KCVn10BpCshtOgUq8/edit?usp=sharing)
- Link to [Slides](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1zQ9mGzs4360IXuUQBVs5kk80xJfrK8QD4VWBos1xXIA/edit?usp=sharing)
![NumPy Newcomers Design Jam_ 2022-09-22 (1)](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46167686/192298868-d4530d4b-62a0-4f8c-877b-819997f6c7d9.png)
![NumPy Newcomers Design Jam_ 2022-09-22 (2)](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46167686/192301087-97cf85e0-6431-405d-acfa-366dd6499967.png)

Audience: Newcomers, volunteer contributors and one maintainer. Different audience from NumPy Community Call on Sept 14: not mostly maintainers.
- **How much math is enough math** to contribute to NumPy?
  - Math in Engineering course in University =/= Math in NumPy
  - Low level vs high level math
- Contributing with **what you already know**
  - Since contributing in free time, limited bandwidth, would like to jump to contributing instead of learning more advanced math... Is this possible? Is there a middle ground? 
- Mars point at the journey map "Unlocks Merge Track" and asked "Do you feel like you are here on this map? Do you feel ready to merge?"
  - Response and Discussion: Maybe. Each project has different needs for merging. Can only learn those needs by being part of community
- NumPy is an mature project: **Effects of project's age on starting contributions**
  - "Feel scared to mess up, cannot live up to several generations of standards"
  - "There is more space to make mistakes in newer projects"
  - The "big wins" already taken years ago
  - Difficult to find a achievable "mission" small enough for newcomers
    - Sprint tagged issues as a mission
    - Flagged optional features for newcomers to "opt in"? 
  - If we acknowledge these fixed properties of NumPy (mature project): Would it be better for a newcomer to open source to start with a downstream project?
    - More important for someone to contribute to open source in general rather than get blocked specifically trying to contribute to NumPy
- Is there a way to **"make NumPy young"** for contributors?
  - Sandbox mode? A separate branch with less pressures?
    - Difficult to implement with release cycles. What is the relation to main branch?
- One volunteer contributors **starting point: GSOD**
  - A **structured** start with clearly defined goals
  - Had **mentors/Point of Contacts** for any question
    - Quicker introduction to NumPy community 
    - Similar to discussion of maintainer bandwidth: How do we let people know we are open for code-reviews/pair programming **while also protecting maintainer bandwidth**?
- How can we **take the positive properties of GSOD to NumPy contributor experience, those who don't start with GSOD**?
  - Maybe Meekail/@Micky774  "Zones of Contribution" is similar, breaking down NumPy into smaller, more digestible blocks?
- Make clearer that **non-code contributions** can be a great place to start
  - Taking meeting notes
    - Inessa and Melissa recently added the line to NumPy community call hack.mds, (on Sept 14) “Notes taken by…” in community meeting notes to credit the work of notetakers –> add “co-authored by” in PRs to numpy/archive.
    - Mars highlighted note-taking and NumPy video transcription in recent keynote
- Bringing in/hiring people with **prior relevant experience instead of "starting from bottom"**
  - Similar to ongoing conversation to role of money and sustaining open source projects
  - Students can't become maintainers, but many newcomers are students. However, it takes a long time/commitment to become a maintainer. Who is keeping their eye on role of "NumPy maintainer" for that long?
  - Bring in people with relevant experience
    - "people from StackExchange with high points in answering X project questions"","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/comments/1258157615/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The alt property as `alt: "…"` is not best practice. Screen readers will read a plot with `alt: "…"` as 'dot, dot, dot'. It is better practice to have empty alt text as `alt: " "`. This allows screen readers to skip the image, the same behavior if the plot had no `alt` property.

In future sprints, we can have the `alt: " "` as a blank space.

However, for this PR, we can merge as is and keep `alt: "…"`. Thank you for writing that alt text for the first time and following up with me in the SciPy slack. 

For submitting the alt text, we could add it via PR or GIthub Suggestions. In the context of these sprints, we are using Github Suggestions so we can centralize everything into this one PR instead of multiple PRs. You will still be get contribution recognition by using Github Suggestions.

Referencing adjacent figures: It depends on the context. If you know for certain that the documentation will change in the near future, such as within a few weeks, you could write alt text as standalone. Some alt text is better than no alt text, and we appreciate the contribution all the same.

However, if the documentation will not change soon and the documentation's images are built on top of each other and directly reference each other (sort of like building layers of a cake), alt text should reference each other. {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/blog.scientific-python.org/pulls/comments/892682860","pull_request_review_id":1000184189,"id":892682860,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOGz9nGM41NUJs","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,71 @@
+---
+title: "Team up! Alt text and cross-project community"
+date: 2022-05-19
+draft: false
+tags: ["Scientific-Python", "event", "acessibility"]
+description: "Learn more about alt text on the blog and how you can contribute."
+displayInList: true
+author: ["Mars Lee", "Isabela Presedo-Floyd"]
+resources:
+  - name: featuredImage
+    src: "alt-text-header.png"
+    params:
+      showOnTop: false
+---
+
+![Icons of the accessibility symbol, a paper tag labeled 'Alt', and a photograph.](alt-text-header.png)
+
+The Scientific Python blog has just gotten a little more accessible! If you 
+didn't catch our [invite on Twitter](https://twitter.com/scientific_py/status/1523733881651834880) 
+or run into the problem firsthand, there's a good chance you might not have 
+noticed the new descriptions for a number of blog post images.
+
+Since it's not a flashy improvement, we wanted to make a point to highlight 
+the community effort to to make a more accessible blog--and internet as a 
+whole--last week.
+
+## What did we do?
+
+In the spirit of [Scientific Python's mission to build community-developed and 
+inclusive spaces](https://scientific-python.org/about/), the project had its 
+first image description workshop on May 13, 2022. We gathered to learn about 
+and practice writing about images from the ground up. Image descriptions--
+[often called `alt text`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLImageElement/alt)--
+are one of many accessibility considerations that people making content are 
+responsible for. 
+[Missing alt text is among the most common culprits of inaccessible content on the internet](https://webaim.org/projects/million/#alttext). 
+Fortunately, missing alt text has a clear fix even for those new to with 
+accessibility: describe the image based on its context.
+
+![The team behind the alt text. Listed from left to right in Zoom, they are.]()
+
+During this event, thirteen people wrote 23 image descriptions to improve 11 
+blog posts in one hour (plus the time for post-event feedback 😉). Wow! The 
+images covered range from illustrative additions, to creative tutorials, to 
+charts that carry the message of the blog post, so it provides great set of 
+examples on how to consider 
+[writing alt text in different situations](https://www.w3.org/WAI/tutorials/images/decision-tree/).
+
+That's not even counting the many questions, discussions, and ephiphanies that 
+happened during out work time. Learning can't be captured as easily by a 
+number, but a taste of our less quantifiable wins can be found on 
+[the workshop recording]().
+
+## Want to learn more?
+
+If you want to further explore the experience and relive the joy, you can find 
+resources on the [event agenda](https://hackmd.io/bfhftUCiTRqx2S8CTGUt6g?view), 
+discussions on the [working pull request](https://github.com/MarsBarLee/blog.scientific-python.org/pull/1), 
+and the final steps needed to make change on the [contributing pull request](https://github.com/scientific-python/blog.scientific-python.org/pull/71).
+
+But wait, there's more! While the community made great progress on improving 
+the blog, the work to add or imporve alt text on Scientific Python's blog, 
+website, and documentation is ongoing. You can continue these efforts by
+
+- Contributing as an individual! There's no reason you can't write some image descriptions for your favorite blog post to make it even better. (👀 This is a great excuse to find a new favorite blog post.)
+- Contributing as a group! Collect your friends, co-workers, neighbors, local book club attendees, or others to team up and write alt text. (Help for duplicating the process of our group PR can be found [on the draft event documentation](https://github.com/isabela-pf/a11y-events/tree/main/workshop-resources/alt-text).)
+- Staying in touch for annoucements of future contributing events on the [Scientific Python Twitter](https://twitter.com/scientific_py).
+
+---
+
+*This image description event has been run across multiple open-source projects in the scientific computing ecosystem. ## What this is

This post summarizes the [image description event](https://twitter.com/scientific_py/status/1523733881651834880) held for the blog earlier in May. It aims to highlight what was done while providing resources for anyone reading to follow up.

## What needs to be done

- [ ] Add recording link
- [x] Add contributing PR link
- [x] Add final number of image descriptions added and posts impacted
- [ ] Someone with relevant knowledge needs to check that the post metadata is all correct
- [ ] Add the group photo we took to line 40. 
- [ ] @MarsBarLee needs to review
- [ ] Update post date when we have an accurate estimate

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/blog.scientific-python.org/pulls/comments/892683093","pull_request_review_id":1000184524,"id":892683093,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOGz9nGM41NUNV","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,71 @@
+---
+title: "Team up! Alt text and cross-project community"
+date: 2022-05-19
+draft: false
+tags: ["Scientific-Python", "event", "acessibility"]
+description: "Learn more about alt text on the blog and how you can contribute."
+displayInList: true
+author: ["Mars Lee", "Isabela Presedo-Floyd"]
+resources:
+  - name: featuredImage
+    src: "alt-text-header.png"
+    params:
+      showOnTop: false
+---
+
+![Icons of the accessibility symbol, a paper tag labeled 'Alt', and a photograph.](alt-text-header.png)
+
+The Scientific Python blog has just gotten a little more accessible! If you 
+didn't catch our [invite on Twitter](https://twitter.com/scientific_py/status/1523733881651834880) 
+or run into the problem firsthand, there's a good chance you might not have 
+noticed the new descriptions for a number of blog post images.
+
+Since it's not a flashy improvement, we wanted to make a point to highlight 
+the community effort to to make a more accessible blog--and internet as a 
+whole--last week.
+
+## What did we do?
+
+In the spirit of [Scientific Python's mission to build community-developed and 
+inclusive spaces](https://scientific-python.org/about/), the project had its 
+first image description workshop on May 13, 2022. We gathered to learn about 
+and practice writing about images from the ground up. Image descriptions--
+[often called `alt text`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLImageElement/alt)--
+are one of many accessibility considerations that people making content are 
+responsible for. 
+[Missing alt text is among the most common culprits of inaccessible content on the internet](https://webaim.org/projects/million/#alttext). 
+Fortunately, missing alt text has a clear fix even for those new to with 
+accessibility: describe the image based on its context.
+
+![The team behind the alt text. Listed from left to right in Zoom, they are.]()
+
+During this event, thirteen people wrote 23 image descriptions to improve 11 
+blog posts in one hour (plus the time for post-event feedback 😉). Wow! The 
+images covered range from illustrative additions, to creative tutorials, to 
+charts that carry the message of the blog post, so it provides great set of 
+examples on how to consider 
+[writing alt text in different situations](https://www.w3.org/WAI/tutorials/images/decision-tree/).
+
+That's not even counting the many questions, discussions, and ephiphanies that 
+happened during out work time. Learning can't be captured as easily by a 
+number, but a taste of our less quantifiable wins can be found on 
+[the workshop recording]().
+
+## Want to learn more?
+
+If you want to further explore the experience and relive the joy, you can find 
+resources on the [event agenda](https://hackmd.io/bfhftUCiTRqx2S8CTGUt6g?view), 
+discussions on the [working pull request](https://github.com/MarsBarLee/blog.scientific-python.org/pull/1), 
+and the final steps needed to make change on the [contributing pull request](https://github.com/scientific-python/blog.scientific-python.org/pull/71).
+
+But wait, there's more! While the community made great progress on improving 
+the blog, the work to add or imporve alt text on Scientific Python's blog, 
+website, and documentation is ongoing. You can continue these efforts by
+
+- Contributing as an individual! There's no reason you can't write some image descriptions for your favorite blog post to make it even better. (👀 This is a great excuse to find a new favorite blog post.)
+- Contributing as a group! Collect your friends, co-workers, neighbors, local book club attendees, or others to team up and write alt text. (Help for duplicating the process of our group PR can be found [on the draft event documentation](https://github.com/isabela-pf/a11y-events/tree/main/workshop-resources/alt-text).)
+- Staying in touch for annoucements of future contributing events on the [Scientific Python Twitter](https://twitter.com/scientific_py).
+
+---
+
+*This image description event has been run across multiple open-source projects in the scientific computing ecosystem. - [ ] Update post date when we have an accurate estimate

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/blog.scientific-python.org/pulls/comments/892683377","pull_request_review_id":1000184914,"id":892683377,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOGz9nGM41NURx","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,71 @@
+---
+title: "Team up! Alt text and cross-project community"
+date: 2022-05-19
+draft: false
+tags: ["Scientific-Python", "event", "acessibility"]
+description: "Learn more about alt text on the blog and how you can contribute."
+displayInList: true
+author: ["Mars Lee", "Isabela Presedo-Floyd"]
+resources:
+  - name: featuredImage
+    src: "alt-text-header.png"
+    params:
+      showOnTop: false
+---
+
+![Icons of the accessibility symbol, a paper tag labeled 'Alt', and a photograph.](alt-text-header.png)
+
+The Scientific Python blog has just gotten a little more accessible! If you 
+didn't catch our [invite on Twitter](https://twitter.com/scientific_py/status/1523733881651834880) 
+or run into the problem firsthand, there's a good chance you might not have 
+noticed the new descriptions for a number of blog post images.
+
+Since it's not a flashy improvement, we wanted to make a point to highlight 
+the community effort to to make a more accessible blog--and internet as a 
+whole--last week.
+
+## What did we do?
+
+In the spirit of [Scientific Python's mission to build community-developed and 
+inclusive spaces](https://scientific-python.org/about/), the project had its 
+first image description workshop on May 13, 2022. We gathered to learn about 
+and practice writing about images from the ground up. Image descriptions--
+[often called `alt text`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLImageElement/alt)--
+are one of many accessibility considerations that people making content are 
+responsible for. 
+[Missing alt text is among the most common culprits of inaccessible content on the internet](https://webaim.org/projects/million/#alttext). 
+Fortunately, missing alt text has a clear fix even for those new to with 
+accessibility: describe the image based on its context.
+
+![The team behind the alt text. Listed from left to right in Zoom, they are.]()
+
+During this event, thirteen people wrote 23 image descriptions to improve 11 
+blog posts in one hour (plus the time for post-event feedback 😉). Wow! The 
+images covered range from illustrative additions, to creative tutorials, to 
+charts that carry the message of the blog post, so it provides great set of 
+examples on how to consider 
+[writing alt text in different situations](https://www.w3.org/WAI/tutorials/images/decision-tree/).
+
+That's not even counting the many questions, discussions, and ephiphanies that 
+happened during out work time. Learning can't be captured as easily by a 
+number, but a taste of our less quantifiable wins can be found on 
+[the workshop recording]().
+
+## Want to learn more?
+
+If you want to further explore the experience and relive the joy, you can find 
+resources on the [event agenda](https://hackmd.io/bfhftUCiTRqx2S8CTGUt6g?view), 
+discussions on the [working pull request](https://github.com/MarsBarLee/blog.scientific-python.org/pull/1), 
+and the final steps needed to make change on the [contributing pull request](https://github.com/scientific-python/blog.scientific-python.org/pull/71).
+
+But wait, there's more! While the community made great progress on improving 
+the blog, the work to add or imporve alt text on Scientific Python's blog, 
+website, and documentation is ongoing. You can continue these efforts by
+
+- Contributing as an individual! There's no reason you can't write some image descriptions for your favorite blog post to make it even better. (👀 This is a great excuse to find a new favorite blog post.)
+- Contributing as a group! Collect your friends, co-workers, neighbors, local book club attendees, or others to team up and write alt text. (Help for duplicating the process of our group PR can be found [on the draft event documentation](https://github.com/isabela-pf/a11y-events/tree/main/workshop-resources/alt-text).)
+- Staying in touch for annoucements of future contributing events on the [Scientific Python Twitter](https://twitter.com/scientific_py).
+
+---
+
+*This image description event has been run across multiple open-source projects in the scientific computing ecosystem. ## What this is

This post summarizes the [image description event](https://twitter.com/scientific_py/status/1523733881651834880) held for the blog earlier in May. It aims to highlight what was done while providing resources for anyone reading to follow up.

## What needs to be done

- [ ] Add recording link
- [x] Add contributing PR link
- [x] Add final number of image descriptions added and posts impacted
- [ ] Someone with relevant knowledge needs to check that the post metadata is all correct
- [ ] Add the group photo we took to line 40. 
- [ ] @MarsBarLee needs to review
- [ ] Update post date when we have an accurate estimate

there y'all. for scipy this year, we wanted to coordinate an effort to further improve the accessibiity of open source documentation. we already had an event with y'all in #208 that felt fun. we wanted to try a similar format, but over the course of the conference. we were wondering if y'all (or another project in your ecosystem) would be interested in holding an issue open to add some more alt text during scipy. we'll be working on directing folks to those prs, and we are imagining that this might be a useful device in workshops/bofs for some light on boarding. our ultimate goal is to make this a scavenger hunt to add alt-text/contribute to multiple projects during the event.

scopes","user":{"login":"isabela-pf","id":50221806,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUwMjIxODA2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/50221806?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/isabela-pf","html_url":"https://github.com/isabela-pf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/isabela-pf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/isabela-pf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/isabela-pf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/isabela-pf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/isabela-pf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/isabela-pf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/isabela-pf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/isabela-pf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/isabela-pf/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-03-31T21:52:57Z","updated_at":"2022-04-06T21:05:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We've been trying to sort out a way to handle the work and scope of this draft SPEC in order to get concrete next steps that benefit all parties' wants and time constraints. Based on our most recent discussion, the next step is to reorganize existing work and fill in the gaps in an outline similar to the following:

# Accessibility 

The SPEC will be about accessibility, even though that is very broad.

## Intro

Scientific Python communities need to be accessible. This SPEC is an agreement between communities to actively make their work more accessible, investigate accessibility solutions where they find gaps in existing recommendations, and share this knowledge and efforts across projects whenever possible. Stuff like that goes here.

## Accessibility standards 

I think this section needs a clearer name.

### Global standards

Accessibility standards already exist, and we respect those first! As far as I know, this section is mainly about WCAG and/or anything else we may want to highlight as comprehensive, non-ecosystem-specific resources.

### Scientific Python recommendations

This section has a bulleted list of specific recommendations in response to the ecosystem's needs.

#### Existing recommendations

- Image descriptions (link to this resource)

#### Future (requested?) recommendations

- Pending

## Community

Who, what, where, when, why, and how to get involved. I do still have some questions here. But I know we want something about this.

---

cc @MarsBarLee since this will be a group effort!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/accessibility.scientific-python.org/issues/4/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/accessibility.scientific-python.org/issues/4/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/accessibility.scientific-python.org/issues/comments/1090806281","html_url":"https://github.com/scientific-python/accessibility.scientific-python.org/issues/4#issuecomment-1090806281","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/accessibility.scientific-python.org/issues/4","id":1090806281,"node_id":"IC_kwDOG1F9Mc5BBGIJ","user":{"login":"MarsBarLee","id":46167686,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTY3Njg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46167686?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee","html_url":"https://github.com/MarsBarLee","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-06T21:05:18Z","updated_at":"2022-04-06T21:05:18Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Dicussion, Feb 6

all,

Thank you for your interest in a possible alt-text workshop! [Here’s an whenisgood to figure out a time](http://whenisgood.net/y5dmit3). Fill in with the times that work for you. The dates range from May 12-13, to allow for some wiggle room. I’ll close it next week.

Based on the current interest, we have people in the Americas and Europe, so around 2pm GMT could be a reasonable time for all. But depends on people’s availability.

In terms of target audience, I assumed that it would be people who responded to this thread and the SPEC discord. However, we can open it to people outside as well! We can advertise it on the SPEC social media. However, if we have people who are unfamiliar with SPEC, we’ll need to account for some time to explain what SPEC is.

Once we have enough responses and a time, I’ll send a follow-up email and Google Meet invite with more information.

Our workshops are usually 60-90 minutes. 60 minutes is the minimum to learn how to write alt-text and start contributing. If anyone needs to leave by then, that’s totally fine. 90 minutes allows some more time to work and have discussion.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/blog.scientific-python.org/issues/comments/1105320115/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

 Contribution Workflow
 ---------------------
+[Link to full alt-text of Contribution Workflow flow chart.](https://gist.githubusercontent.com/MarsBarLee/2dc5e9c83adf0555d08277b2262302e5/raw/be6a0c46f58d7135a75df7747698cef676b9cdc0/Jupyter%2520Documentation:%2520Contribution%2520Workflow%2520Alt-Text)","path":"docs/source/contributing/dev-contributions/contrib_guide.rst","position":4,"original_position":4,"commit_id":"7047ec554b26952def58c00e09613e5db9453db1","original_commit_id":"7047ec554b26952def58c00e09613e5db9453db1","user":{"login":"MarsBarLee","id":46167686,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTY3Njg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46167686?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee","html_url":"https://github.com/MarsBarLee","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MarsBarLee/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"For this alt-text, I provided both short alt-text and long alt-text. In this context, longer alt-text is valuable as short-alt text would be excluding essential information in this flowchart. I have linked out to the long alt-text.

During the workshop, I linked to a personal gist due to the ease of use and speed. However, for submission into the Jupyter documentation, I think linking to a markdown file in the Jupyter documentation would be better. 

Where would a markdown file with an image's long text be contained? In this case, the file is in contributing/dev-contributions but the image is in _static/_images. I'm leaning towards putting it in the images folder. The markdown file could be titled 'contribution_workflow_long_alt_text.md' so it would show next to the image 'contribution_workflow.png'.

In reference to [WCAG](https://www.w3.org/WAI/tutorials/images/complex/#a-text-link-to-the-long-description-adjacent-to-the-image) and [Deque](https://dequeuniversity.com/tips/long-descriptions), both refer to linking out to long alt-text, but do not advice where the text should be hosted.

I could create a separate PR after this PR submitted to change the link and add the long alt-text PR adds or updates existing [alt text](https://moz.com/learn/seo/alt-text) to Project Jupyter documentation. It includes alt text for
- all images on the main documentation
- all images used in community call notes

If there are images in this repo you think were missed, I'd love to know so we can address them in the future.

If you have any questions about reviewing alt text, let me know and I'd be happy to tell you what I look for.

Thanks in advance for reviewing this PR! 🌻 

---

This alt text was contributed by wonderful volunteers at one of the [Jupyter accessibility workshops](https://blog.jupyter.org/join-us-for-the-jupyter-accessibility-workshops-part-1-133e0e522d1b)! They teamed up to make progress on so many images. 

If you are curious how we have a commit authored by ten people (wow! 🎉), feel free to check out our [working PR](https://github.com/isabela-pf/jupyter/pull/1) or the [How to Participate section of the event agenda](https://github.com/Quansight-Labs/jupyter-accessibility-workshops/blob/main/events/2022-january-22/mentored_sprint_agenda.md#how-to-participate-in-writing-alt-text).

PR adds or updates existing [alt text](https://moz.com/learn/seo/alt-text) to Project Jupyter documentation. It includes alt text for
- all images on the main documentation
- all images used in community call notes

If there are images in this repo you think were missed, I'd love to know so we can address them in the future.

If you have any questions about reviewing alt text, let me know and I'd be happy to tell you what I look for.

Thanks in advance for reviewing this PR! 🌻 

---

This alt text was contributed by wonderful volunteers at one of the [Jupyter accessibility workshops](https://blog.jupyter.org/join-us-for-the-jupyter-accessibility-workshops-part-1-133e0e522d1b)! They teamed up to make progress on so many images. 

If you are curious how we have a commit authored by ten people (wow! 🎉), feel free to check out our [working PR](https://github.com/isabela-pf/jupyter/pull/1) or the [How to Participate section of the event agenda](https://github.com/Quansight-Labs/jupyter-accessibility-workshops/blob/main/events/2022-january-22/mentored_sprint_agenda.md#how-to-participate-in-writing-alt-text).
